title: Positive input ventilation explained
description: How a positive input ventilation (PIV) unit stops condensation and mould, what it costs to buy, install and run, and how it compares with a dehumidifier.
blurb: What a PIV unit does, what it costs to install and run, and whether it beats a dehumidifier for condensation.
lede: Positive input ventilation, usually shortened to PIV, is the quiet box in the loft that surveyors, landlords and damp specialists keep recommending for condensation and mould. Instead of sucking damp air out of one room the way an extractor fan does, it gently pushes filtered fresh air into the whole house from a central point, raising the indoor air pressure just enough that stale, moist air gets squeezed out through the natural gaps every home has. It is one of the cheapest whole-house ventilation systems to run, often under £20 a year in electricity, and for a house that wakes up to streaming windows every winter morning it can make a dramatic difference. This guide covers how PIV works, what it costs to buy, install and run, where it falls short, and how it stacks up against running a dehumidifier instead.
short_answer: A PIV unit pushes filtered air from your loft into the house, displacing damp air and cutting condensation. The fan draws only 5 to 15 watts, so continuous running costs roughly £11 to £34 a year at 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026). The unit itself costs around £250 to £450, with typical installed prices of £600 to £1,000, and the optional pre-heater adds £25 to £40 a year in winter if you use it.

sections.h2: How positive input ventilation actually works
sections.html:
A standard PIV unit is a fan and filter assembly mounted in the loft, connected to a ceiling diffuser on the landing or central hallway. It draws air from the loft space, passes it through a filter, and pushes it gently down into the house around the clock. Because more air is coming in than the house would otherwise admit, the indoor pressure rises very slightly above the pressure outside. That small positive pressure forces the existing air, along with the moisture it carries, out through trickle vents, gaps around windows and doors, extractor outlets and all the other small leakage paths a normal house has.

The result is a continuous, slow change of air across the whole property rather than in one room. Moist air generated by cooking, showers, drying laundry and simply breathing never gets the chance to sit still, cool down and condense on cold surfaces. That is why PIV has a strong track record against condensation and the black mould that follows it, particularly in bedrooms and corners that extractor fans never reach.

A few practical points worth knowing:

  • Loft air is used because it is free to access and, for much of the year, slightly warmer than outside air thanks to solar gain on the roof and heat rising from the rooms below.
  • Flats and houses without a usable loft can use a wall-mounted PIV unit instead, which draws air through an external wall.
  • Most units sense temperature and throttle back or stop when the loft gets hot in summer, so you are not pumping warm air in during a heatwave.
  • Many models offer an optional integral pre-heater of around 400 to 500 watts that takes the chill off incoming air on cold days. It is a comfort feature, not a heating system, and it is the only part of a PIV unit that costs real money to run.

sections.h2: What a PIV unit costs to buy and install
sections.html:
PIV is one of the cheaper whole-house ventilation options to get installed. Typical UK prices look like this:

  • The unit itself: around £250 to £450 for a mainstream loft-mounted model. Wall-mounted versions for flats tend to sit at the upper end or slightly above it.
  • Professional installation: commonly £200 to £500 on top, so a typical all-in installed price of £600 to £1,000. The job involves mounting the unit in the loft, cutting a ceiling aperture for the diffuser, and wiring it to a fused spur.
  • DIY: the mechanical side is well within reach of a confident DIYer, but the electrical connection should be done by an electrician unless you are competent to sign it off. Budget £80 to £150 for that visit.

Placement matters more than brand. The diffuser should sit in the most central circulation space, usually the upstairs landing in a two-storey house, so the incoming air can reach every room as internal doors open and close. Bedrooms with the doors permanently shut will see less benefit, so undercutting doors by a centimetre or fitting door vents helps the air move.

If your problem is mould in one specific room rather than condensation everywhere, read our guide to cheap fixes for condensation and mould first. A £400 ventilation system is the right answer to a whole-house problem, not to a single cold corner behind a wardrobe, which is often solved for a few pounds.

sections.h2: What a PIV unit costs to run
sections.html:
This is where PIV shines. The fan is tiny, comparable to a broadband router, and it is designed to run continuously. At 26.11p per kWh, every watt running around the clock costs about £2.29 a year, which makes the sums easy.

SettingTypical powerkWh per year (24/7)Cost per year
Trickle speed, smaller house5 W44£11
Standard speed8 W70£18
Higher speed, larger house15 W131£34
Boost25 W219£57
Optional pre-heater, winter use only400 to 500 W100 to 150£26 to £39

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem electricity price cap, July to September 2026. Fan figures assume continuous running at the stated power. Pre-heater estimate assumes one to two hours of operation a day across the coldest four months, which varies with the temperature you set it to.

So a realistic total for most households is £15 to £35 a year for the fan, plus £25 to £40 if you enable the pre-heater through winter. Even with the heater on, the whole system usually costs less per year than many people spend per month running a dehumidifier hard in winter. There are no consumables beyond the filter, which on most models needs checking every couple of years and replacing roughly every five, at £20 to £40 a time.

sections.h2: PIV versus a dehumidifier versus extractor fans
sections.html:
These three tools all fight moisture, but they do different jobs and the running costs are worlds apart.

Dehumidifier

A compressor dehumidifier pulls water out of the air in the room it sits in. A typical 200 watt model running eight hours a day uses about 1.6 kWh, which is roughly 42p a day or £12 to £13 a month at the current cap. Over a five-month damp season that is £60 or more, every year, for one machine covering part of the house. The full breakdown is in our dehumidifier running cost guide. Dehumidifiers win when you need fast, targeted drying, for example a room where laundry dries, a flood recovery, or a rented home where you cannot drill holes in the ceiling.

Extractor fans

Kitchen and bathroom extractors remove moisture at the source before it spreads, and they remain essential whether or not you fit PIV. They cost pennies to run but only protect the room they are in.

PIV

PIV treats the whole house at once for £15 to £35 a year, but it works slowly and gently. It will not dry a soaked carpet, and it cannot fix moisture that comes from a building defect such as a leaking roof, failed pointing or rising damp. It manages the moisture that everyday living puts into the air, which is the cause of the great majority of UK condensation and mould complaints.

A common and sensible pattern: keep the extractors, fit PIV for the background problem, and keep a dehumidifier only if you dry laundry indoors in one room. Many PIV owners find they stop using the dehumidifier entirely, which means the system effectively pays its running cost several times over in its first winter.

sections.h2: Will PIV push up your heating bill?
sections.html:
It is a fair worry. PIV deliberately increases the amount of outside air moving through your home, and in winter that air has to be heated. Three things keep the penalty small in practice:

  • The air is pre-warmed for free. Loft air is typically a few degrees warmer than outside air during daylight hours, because the roof acts as a crude solar collector and heat escaping the rooms below collects there.
  • Dry air is cheaper to heat and feels warmer. Damp air takes more energy to warm, and damp walls conduct heat away faster than dry ones. Drying the fabric of the building out is a genuine efficiency gain, which is why homes with bad condensation often feel cold even with the heating running. If that sounds familiar, our heating hub covers the wider picture.
  • The flow is modest. A PIV unit on a normal setting moves a gentle trickle of air, far less than leaving a window cracked open all day.

Independent assessments generally put the net heating impact at small to negligible for a typical home, and some occupants report feeling warmer because the damp has gone. The diffuser itself can create a noticeable cool spot directly beneath it on cold days, which is what the pre-heater option exists to soften.

One thing PIV does not change: draught-proofing is still worth doing. PIV needs leakage paths to push air out through, but it works best when the air leaves through deliberate openings such as trickle vents rather than howling gaps under doors. Controlled ventilation plus sealed accidental draughts is the combination to aim for, and our guide to ventilation without losing heat explains how the two fit together.

PIV is one of the rare home improvements where the running cost is almost too small to matter. For £600 to £1,000 installed and £15 to £35 a year in electricity, you get continuous whole-house ventilation that deals with the root cause of condensation rather than mopping up the symptoms. If you are currently feeding a dehumidifier £10 or more a month through winter, a PIV unit can pay back the difference in running costs within a few years while doing a more complete job.

It is the right tool when the problem is condensation and mould across multiple rooms in a reasonably sound house. It is the wrong tool for building defects, for single-room problems that a £10 fix would solve, and for homes where the real issue is unheated rooms with no air movement at all. Get the cheap basics right first, then let a 7 watt fan in the loft quietly handle the rest.

faq.q: Does positive input ventilation work in a flat without a loft?
faq.a: Yes. Wall-mounted PIV units draw air through an external wall instead of the loft and work on the same principle. They cost slightly more than loft models and lose the free pre-warming that loft air provides, so the pre-heater option is more useful in a flat.

faq.q: Should a PIV unit run all the time?
faq.a: Yes. PIV is designed for continuous operation and only works while it is running, because the positive pressure disappears the moment the fan stops. At 5 to 15 watts it costs between 3p and 9p a day, so switching it off overnight saves almost nothing and lets condensation return.

faq.q: How long does a PIV unit take to stop condensation?
faq.a: Most households see windows clearing within days, but allow two to six weeks for the full effect because the fabric of the house, including plaster and furnishings, holds moisture that takes time to dry out. Existing mould still needs cleaning off; PIV stops it coming back rather than removing what is there.

faq.q: Does a PIV unit make the house cold?
faq.a: You may feel a cool airflow directly under the diffuser on cold days, which the optional pre-heater is designed to soften. Across the house the net heating impact is small, and because dry air and dry walls hold heat better, many owners report the home feels warmer once the damp has gone.

faq.q: How often does a PIV filter need changing?
faq.a: Check the filter every year or two and expect to replace it roughly every five years on most mainstream models, at around £20 to £40. A clogged filter makes the fan work harder and cuts the airflow, so it is worth a diary reminder.
